Reading netCDF files / HDF files / opendap servers¶

SemperPy provides tools to read netCDF 3.x and 4.x files, hdf 4.x and 5.x files and access opendap servers transparently thanks to the netcdf4 library which handles all these formats. Thanks to slicing and the support given by slicing.dimensions and slicing.cf10, it becomes easy to slice variables read from those files:

Reading into multi-dimensional numpy arrays¶

This method matches the way data is sliced and returned in grads (pygrads), this is more useful in interactive mode.

import os
from semperpy.netcdf.file import File
import gmaopy.netcdf # register dimensions specific to our files

datapath = os.getenv('SEMPERPY_TESTDATA')
reader = File(datapath + '/an.hdf')
directive = dict(
    variable = 't',
    date = 2010071000,
    level = 500
)
w = reader.readArray(directive)
print
print "shape: %s, mean: %f" % (w.shape, w.mean())

Download example 1

The output shows:

shape: (1, 1, 361, 540), mean: 255.139215

No latitudes or longitudes are specified, therefore the global field is returned (361,340). Subareas can be specified:

import os
from semperpy.netcdf.file import File
import gmaopy.netcdf # register dimensions specific to our files

datapath = os.getenv('SEMPERPY_TESTDATA')
reader = File(datapath + '/an.hdf')
directive = dict(
    variable = 't',
    date = 2010071000,
    latitude = {20:90},
    longitude = {120:250},
    level = 500
)
w = reader.readArray(directive)
print "shape: %s, mean: %f" % (w.shape, w.mean())

Download example 2

The output shows:

(1, 1, 141, 196), mean: 259.202858

No latitudes or longitudes are specified, therefore the global field is returned (361,340). Only one level is specified, more than level can be specified:

import os
from semperpy.netcdf.file import File
import gmaopy.netcdf # register dimensions specific to our files

datapath = os.getenv('SEMPERPY_TESTDATA')
reader = File(datapath + '/an.hdf')
directive = dict(
    variable = 't',
    date = 2010071000,
    latitude = {20:90},
    longitude = {120:250},
    level = [1000,500]
)
w = reader.readArray(directive)
print "shape: %s, mean: %f" % (w.shape, w.mean())

Download example 3

The output shows:

shape: (1, 2, 141, 196), mean: 269.782462

Two levels have now been returned. It can sometimes be useful to request “all available levels” between two values. A list describes discrete values [a,b,c,d], [~a,~b,~c,~d] is returned where ~ means closest value. A dictionary describes an interval {a:b}, [>=a,...,<=b] is returned including all values between a and b:

import os
from semperpy.netcdf.file import File
import gmaopy.netcdf # register dimensions specific to our files

datapath = os.getenv('SEMPERPY_TESTDATA')
reader = File(datapath + '/an.hdf')
directive = dict(
    variable = 't',
    date = 2010071000,
    latitude = {20:90},
    longitude = {120:250},
    level = {1000:500}
)
w = reader.readArray(directive)
print "shape: %s, mean: %f" % (w.shape, w.mean())

Download example 4

The output shows:

shape: (1, 15, 141, 196), mean: 276.723312

There are 15 levels available in the file: 1000, 975, 950, 925, 900, 875, 850, 825, 800, 750, 700, 650, 600, 550, 500.

import os
from semperpy.netcdf.file import File
import gmaopy.netcdf # register dimensions specific to our files

datapath = os.getenv('SEMPERPY_TESTDATA')
reader = File(datapath + '/an.hdf')
directive = dict(
    variable = 't',
    date = 2010071000,
    latitude = 0,
    level = {1000:0} # all levels, could omit this line
)
w = reader.readArray(directive)
print "shape: %s, mean: %f" % (w.shape, w.mean())

Download example 5

The output shows:

shape: (1, 36, 1, 540), mean: 254.547819

This is a vertical slice along the equator.

Reading into a collection of fields¶

For some applications it can be more practical to read data into a collection of objects:

import os
from semperpy.netcdf.file import File
import gmaopy.netcdf # register dimensions specific to our files

datapath = os.getenv('SEMPERPY_TESTDATA')
reader = File(datapath + '/an.hdf')
directive = dict(
    variable = 't',
    date = 2010071000,
    level = {1000:500}
)
w = reader.readBundle(directive)
print
print len(w)
print
for field in w:
    print field.shape()
    print field.get('level')
    print field.get('date')
    print

Download example 1

The output shows:

1

(15, 361, 540)
[1000.0, 975.0, 950.0, 925.0, 900.0, 875.0, 850.0, 825.0, 800.0, 750.0, 700.0, 650.0, 600.0, 550.0, 500.0]
2010-07-10 00

The bundle (fieldset) contains a 3D field. Once again, if levels are specified as discrete values:

import os
from semperpy.netcdf.file import File
import gmaopy.netcdf # register dimensions specific to our files

datapath = os.getenv('SEMPERPY_TESTDATA')
reader = File(datapath + '/an.hdf')
directive = dict(
    variable = 't',
    date = 2010071000,
    level = [1000,500]
)
w = reader.readBundle(directive)
print
print len(w)
print
for field in w:
    print field.shape()
    print field.get('level')
    print field.get('date')
    print

Download example 2

The output shows:

2

(361, 540)
1000.0
2010-07-10 00

(361, 540)
500.0
2010-07-10 00

We now obtain a two 2D field bundle. However, a bundle could very well contain fields which are vertical slices:

import os
from semperpy.netcdf.file import File
import gmaopy.netcdf # register dimensions specific to our files

datapath = os.getenv('SEMPERPY_TESTDATA')
reader = File(datapath + '/an.hdf')
directive = dict(
    variable = 't',
    date = 2010071000,
    level = {1000:500},
    latitude = [0,10,20,30,40,50]
)
w = reader.readBundle(directive)
print
print len(w)
print
for field in w:
    print field.shape()
    print "level %s" % field.get('level')
    print "latitude %f" % field.get('latitude')
    print

Download example 3

The output shows:

6

(15, 540)
level [1000.0, 975.0, 950.0, 925.0, 900.0, 875.0, 850.0, 825.0, 800.0, 750.0, 700.0, 650.0, 600.0, 550.0, 500.0]
latitude 0.000000

(15, 540)
level [1000.0, 975.0, 950.0, 925.0, 900.0, 875.0, 850.0, 825.0, 800.0, 750.0, 700.0, 650.0, 600.0, 550.0, 500.0]
latitude 10.000000

(15, 540)
level [1000.0, 975.0, 950.0, 925.0, 900.0, 875.0, 850.0, 825.0, 800.0, 750.0, 700.0, 650.0, 600.0, 550.0, 500.0]
latitude 20.000000

(15, 540)
level [1000.0, 975.0, 950.0, 925.0, 900.0, 875.0, 850.0, 825.0, 800.0, 750.0, 700.0, 650.0, 600.0, 550.0, 500.0]
latitude 30.000000

(15, 540)
level [1000.0, 975.0, 950.0, 925.0, 900.0, 875.0, 850.0, 825.0, 800.0, 750.0, 700.0, 650.0, 600.0, 550.0, 500.0]
latitude 40.000000

(15, 540)
level [1000.0, 975.0, 950.0, 925.0, 900.0, 875.0, 850.0, 825.0, 800.0, 750.0, 700.0, 650.0, 600.0, 550.0, 500.0]
latitude 50.000000

Just the same way, we can access MERRA data through the opendap server:

from semperpy.core.date import Dates
from semperpy.netcdf.file import File
import gmaopy.netcdf # register dimensions specific to our files

reader = File('http://goldsmr3.sci.gsfc.nasa.gov:80/dods/MAI3CPASM')
directive = dict(
    variable = 'u',
    date = Dates(2001010100,2001013118,6),
    level = 850,
)
w = reader.readBundle(directive)
print
print len(w)

Download example 4

The output shows:

124

The advantage in reading bundle is that fields are created without loading their data. This makes the reading faster and enables to read a large number of fields without using any memory. Memory is used as soon as field.values() is called, but when you are done with a field you can call field.unload().

Table Of Contents

  • Reading netCDF files / HDF files / opendap servers

    • Reading into a collection of fields
